nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; inhabits sandy and rocky bottoms, crevices and ledges of seaward reefs at depths typically 3–50 m, most often shallower than 4 m.

decorPreferences: large caves and crevices sized to conceal the entire body
stable live rock structure (secure rockwork to prevent collapses)
tight-fitting, weighted lid with no gaps

diet.notes: Specialized crustacean predator with close-set pebble-like teeth adapted for crushing hard-shelled prey. Has been documented eating xanthid crabs, shrimp, squid, clams, scallops, mollusks, and sea urchins. In captivity, generally accepts meaty frozen and fresh foods (shrimp, squid, clams, fish flesh); live food may be needed initially for newly wild-caught individuals. Feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is advised to avoid accidental bites. A large meal every few days tends to be preferable to frequent small feedings.

goodWith: aggressive fish (not small enough to swallow)
avoidWith: all crustaceans (shrimp, crabs, lobsters — will be eaten)
very small fish (risk of accidental ingestion)

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5653x3769. Sharp, well-lit in-situ shot of a single live specimen with crisp white zebra banding on a dark body and blunt snout clearly shown; best diagnostic + quality pick.
Jean-Paul Boerekamps (via iNaturalist/GBIF), 2048x1938. Clean, watermark-free sharp head close-up showing the pale banding and pebble-toothed jaw; different individual for variety.
Diego Delso, 4711x3141. Sharp, vibrant reef shot; the banded zebra moray is the clear foreground subject (a spotted tiger moray is visible behind, caveat).

summary: The Zebra Moray (Gymnomuraena zebra) is generally considered one of the more tractable moray species for the home aquarium, known for its distinctive dark-banded pattern and blunt crustacean-crushing teeth. It tends to be peaceful toward fish tankmates but will eat any crustaceans present.
careNotes:
The Zebra Moray is generally considered among the more aquarium-suitable morays due to its peaceful disposition toward fish and its specialized diet of hard-shelled invertebrates — blunt, pebble-like teeth mean it poses little tearing risk to fish tankmates. It has been reported to reach up to 150 cm (about 5 ft) in the wild, though most captive specimens tend to stay in the 60–90 cm range. A 125-gallon (473 L) aquarium is commonly cited as the minimum for a single specimen, but full-grown adults may benefit from 250–264 gallons (950–1000 L) or more, especially when kept with tankmates.

A tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential — this species has been documented pushing loose covers aside and escaping, and such incidents are well-documented across the hobby. Stable, well-secured rockwork is equally important, as the eel tends to rearrange substrate and can topple unsecured structures.

Reef safety is nuanced: the Zebra Moray will consume any crustaceans in the tank (shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs, lobsters) and large specimens may accidentally damage corals or gorgonians through sheer bulk and movement. For this reason it is generally advised to keep it only with non-crustacean invertebrates, though corals are not actively targeted.

Aquarium hobby sources and wild-habitat data differ slightly on temperature: the hobby commonly recommends 72–78°F (22–26°C), while wild populations have been recorded across a somewhat warmer range, and both data points are worth keeping in mind when setting up the tank.

The Zebra Moray is not considered venomous; bites can be painful due to jaw strength but do not involve venom. Feeding should always be done with tongs or a feeding stick rather than by hand.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented. Captive reproduction is generally considered not achieved under typical aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page confirmed: care level moderate, temperament peaceful, reef safe with caution, 125 gal minimum, 72-78°F, SG 1.020-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (150 cm max), taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), native range, wild temperature range (24.7–29.1°C, higher than hobby sources), diet (xanthid crabs and crustaceans). Wild temperature data differs from hobby recommendation — averaged in waterParameters.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (72-78°F / 22-26°C), salinity (1.022-1.025), pH (8.1-8.4), adult size (150 cm), carnivore diet. Suggested 250 gal with tankmates. Noted escape-artist behavior requiring weighted lid.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-verified adult size (150 cm), minimum tank (1000 L for adults), SG ~1.026, reef-safe-with-caution status. Confirmed escape-artist and rockwork-rearranging behaviors.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding has been reported. Sex change has been noted in the wild, though the details remain unconfirmed in a captive context. Reproduction under typical aquarium conditions is not considered achievable at present.
